focus: Resolve seam continuity with minimal edits.
next_head_excerpt:
# Chapter 9

The Bebop in fuel-save mode sounded different. With the engines down to a careful sip and half the lights cut, the ship stopped pretending to be dignified and let its old bones speak. Coolant ticked in the wall behind the galley. A vent fan worried the same dry bearing every seventh turn. The refrigerator gave a low irritated hum, quit, thought about it, and tried again.

Ed liked the stripped-down version of the ship. Fewer noises meant the hidden ones came forward.
Thin red dust still striped the floor by the couch where Spike had come in before dawn and stood for a while with one hand inside his jacket.

Data made noise too. Not to everybody. To Jet, a screen was a screen until it told him whether he could afford noodles. To Spike, a screen was something you glanced at for exits and trouble and then ignored on purpose. To Faye, a screen was debt, odds, a bill, a face, a lie. To Ed, the different systems all had their own little songs. Bounty archives were stiff and papery, all rustle and clack, like somebody riffling old cards with dry fingers. Port authority ledgers had a tinny beep to them, money-counting sounds. Hospital back-end systems lived in cold blue clicks. Obsolete syndicate freight code came in greasy, low, and red, the kind of sound that stuck to a wrench.

Tonight the songs were everywhere.

Ed had turned the common room floor into a nest. Two portable monitors from the engine room leaned against the couch. One stolen galley stool held a projector. Three hand terminals were spread open in a circle around Ed's bare feet. Thin cables ran under the table, over the arm of the couch, through the handle of a pot Jet had not found missing yet, and into the access panel by the main screen. Ein lay under the table with his nose on Ed's ankle, eyes m...
prev_tail_excerpt:
...o bruised violet while the cold started coming down. Faye opened the certified log again and turned past the summary to the denser pages with the bad toner and the certification stamp half bitten off at one corner.

Page four gave her two lines the headline hadn't. One from Tharsis: DUST RING ROOMS / identity verification passed / cash deposit waived. Another from seven months later: LOW MESA CLINIC / intake confirmed / claim denied, prior balance carried. The file didn't know a face or a reason. It knew a room, a clinic desk, a debt that kept following her name long after it stopped belonging to her alone.

She turned to the rights notice. Provider identifiers. Transit-gate images. Signed applications. Separate fee each. She slid that page into the inside pocket of her jacket instead of putting it back on the stack. Jet could ask practical questions later. Spike could go quiet later. Tonight the paper was hers. Everybody else could wait.

Her hand rose toward her collarbone and stopped. That stupid little touch was still the part she could not get past. If the other woman had been using the file years before Faye woke, then Faye had not learned the gesture from her in any ordinary way. Either it belonged to the lost version of Faye from before the ice, or habit lived somewhere memory could not reach, which was worse.

She reached back through the fabric of her top to the smooth skin over her left shoulder blade. Nothing. No raised line, no seam to match Miriam's memory. This body was hers. Those eleven years were not. The name sat between them anyway.

Outside, the city had gone full dark. Inside, the office kept humming around her. She did not cry. She held the top page flat with one hand and the unmarked place on her back with the other and let the vertigo stay ugly.
